questions.question: A pressure-decay test on a completed hydraulic manifold fails its total-leakage acceptance limit. In a mature leak testing program, the next step is to:
options: Repeat the decay test with a longer hold time until the manifold passes
Apply a locating technique — bubble solution or a sniffer probe — to find the individual leaks, repair them, and retest
Switch to a helium hood test to obtain a more sensitive total-leakage number
Reject the manifold outright, since a failed total cannot be investigated further
questions.explanation: Totaling techniques like pressure decay prove compliance with one number but point at nothing. Mature programs run the pair: total first, and when the total fails, a locating technique finds what to fix — then repair and retest. A more sensitive total (the hood) would only re-measure what is already known, and re-running the same test until it passes proves nothing.

questions.question: Helium is the near-universal tracer for mass spectrometer leak testing because it:
options: Is abundant in the atmosphere, guaranteeing a strong natural signal at every leak
Is the heaviest of the inert gases, so it lingers near the leak instead of dispersing
Reads cleanly at mass 4, is rare in air (about 5 ppm) for low background, and is inert, small, and fast
Reacts chemically with the detector's heated platinum anode to produce the ion current
questions.explanation: The spectrometer is tuned to mass 4, which no other common species shares; helium's 5 ppm atmospheric rarity keeps the background floor low; and because it is inert, nontoxic, small, and fast, it slips through fine passages and pumps away quickly. Abundance would be a defect, not a virtue — and the heated anode that responds chemically to tracer gas is the halogen diode, not the MSLD.

questions.question: An inspection planner schedules a hydrostatic proof test and a penetrant inspection of a weldment BEFORE its helium fine-leak test. The leak testing objection to this sequence is that:
options: Liquids from those tests can plug the fine leak passages, and a plugged leak is an invisible leak
Hydrostatic pressure permanently enlarges the leaks, making the helium test read falsely high
Penetrant dye fluoresces at mass 4 and masks the helium signal
Water raises the atmospheric helium background above 5 ppm
questions.explanation: Water, oil, penetrant fluids, and residues can block a fine passage completely, so the part passes the helium test while the leak is merely plugged. Parts are leak tested clean and dry, and the most sensitive test is sequenced onto the cleanest part — before paint, before hydro, and before any liquid-based examination wets the passages being hunted.

questions.question: During a pneumatic leak test the nitrogen supply runs out, and a technician proposes finishing pressurization from an oxygen cylinder. The correct response is:
options: Proceed, since the relief device is already set at the test pressure
Proceed, because oxygen itself is nonflammable and the system holds no fuel
Proceed, but pressurize in smaller steps and read the gauges from a distance
Refuse — oxygen is never used as a pressurizing or tracer gas because it reacts violently with oils and greases
questions.explanation: Oxygen discipline is absolute in leak testing: it reacts violently with the oil and grease films present in ordinary equipment, so it never serves as a test gas. Relief protection, stepped pressurization, and distance are essential pneumatic practices, but none of them makes oxygen acceptable — and 'nonflammable' is the trap, since oxygen drives combustion of everything around it.

questions.question: An MSLD is connected to a 240-liter vacuum chamber evacuated at an effective pumping speed of 8 L/s. After helium spray reaches a leak, the signal climbs to about 63% of its final value in approximately:
options: 3 seconds
30 seconds — the time constant τ = V/S
90 seconds
No time at all; mass spectrometer response is instantaneous
questions.explanation: The response time constant is τ = V/S = 240 L ÷ 8 L/s = 30 s, with the signal near 63% of final after one time constant and about 95% after three (90 s). Practically, the spray probe must dwell and the operator must wait at least one τ per joint, or the leak's answer arrives after the probe has moved on.

questions.question: In a Method D (post-emulsifiable hydrophilic) penetrant line, the coarse water pre-rinse applied before the emulsifier serves to:
options: Dilute the hydrophilic emulsifier down to its 35% working concentration
Flush penetrant out of the flaw openings so the emulsifier can finish removal inside them
Strip the bulk surface penetrant so the dilute emulsifier acts only on the thin layer that remains
Wet the surface so the lipophilic emulsifier spreads evenly when dipped
questions.explanation: The pre-rinse knocks off most of the surface penetrant, leaving the dilute detergent emulsifier (35% maximum for immersion, 5% for spray) only a thin film to make washable. The entrapped penetrant inside a flaw never meets the emulsifier — that is the heart of Method D's overwash resistance and why it processes critical rotating aerospace hardware. Lipophilic dipping belongs to Method B.

questions.question: Which developer form is applied BEFORE the part goes into the drying oven?
options: Dry powder, dusted on so the oven can bake it into place
Nonaqueous wet developer, so the solvent flashes off during drying
Water-suspendible (or water-soluble) developer, applied as a dip or spray on the still-wet part
None — every developer form requires a fully dried part first
questions.explanation: The sequence nuance: aqueous developers — water-soluble and water-suspendible — go on before oven drying, while dry-powder and nonaqueous (solvent-suspendible) developers require a clean, dry part. Reversing the order either washes the aqueous coating's purpose away or traps moisture under the dry forms.

questions.question: Under AMS 2644, penetrant system sensitivity is classified as follows:
options: Fluorescent systems are graded from level ½ (ultralow) through level 4 (ultrahigh); visible systems carry a single level
Visible systems are graded ½ through 4; fluorescent systems carry a single level
Both families are graded 1 through 10 according to dye brightness
Sensitivity level is set entirely by the developer form selected, not the penetrant
questions.explanation: AMS 2644 grades fluorescent penetrant systems from level ½ to level 4, while visible (Type II) systems carry one level — a reminder of why fluorescent dominates critical aerospace work. Higher sensitivity is not free: brighter, tighter-clinging dyes also light up rough surfaces and processing residue, so procedures match the level to the hardware's criticality and finish.

questions.question: After development, an indication measures 6 mm long and 1.5 mm wide. Under typical written acceptance criteria it is classified as:
options: Rounded, because it is wider than 1 mm
Linear, because its length is more than three times its width
Nonrelevant, because developer bleed-out exaggerates indication size
Linear only if it can first be confirmed to be a crack
questions.explanation: Most criteria call an indication linear when length exceeds three times width — here 6:1.5 is 4:1 — and rounded otherwise, and that classification decides which acceptance limit applies. Because bleed-out exaggerates width, criteria work mostly from length; the magnification makes the flaw visible but does not make the indication nonrelevant.

questions.question: A visible-dye field inspection must be performed on a flange sitting at 30 °F. Standard practice requires the technician to:
options: Proceed normally, since dye penetrant performance is independent of temperature
Double the dwell time, which automatically compensates for any temperature
Either bring the joint into the roughly 40–125 °F (4–52 °C) window or use a technique separately qualified for the low temperature, such as with comparator blocks
Switch to a water-washable penetrant, which is rated for sub-freezing work
questions.explanation: Standard penetrant processing applies between about 40 and 125 °F (4–52 °C); 30 °F sits outside the window, where the technique must be separately qualified — comparator blocks are the classic route — or the part warmed into range. Longer dwell helps cold parts within the window but does not substitute for qualification outside it.
